result:
I want to write about the moment the screen goes dark after you close the laptop and the world rushes back in through your ears like a tide you forgot was rising. That hush is never empty; it is full of refrigerator hum, faraway sirens, a neighbor’s dog rehearsing the same three-note complaint it’s delivered since 2019. Nothing has changed, yet everything feels edited, as if your brain has just switched codecs and the color grading of reality is suddenly more honest: the warm white LED bulbs you bought to fight seasonal depression now look institutional, the pile of laundry on the chair a topo map of the week you swore you’d finally get ahead.  

I want to write about how the internet used to feel like a library built at the edge of town, open twenty-four hours, staffed by volunteers who wanted you to discover Rainer Maria Rilke and 8-bit remixes of Bach in the same night. Now it feels like a mall that’s always having a going-out-of-business sale, the fluorescent lights flickering at a frequency scientifically calculated to make you reach for your wallet before your conscience catches up. I still walk every aisle, though, running my thumb across merchandise that dissolves on contact: a thirty-second video of a raccoon eating a slice of watermelon, an influencer crying about her divorce while selling vitamin gummies, an ad for the same shoes I already bought, now thirty percent cheaper, a bargain I confuse with betrayal.  

I want to write about the time my father tried to teach me how to whittle. We sat on the back porch in July, citronella candles guarding us like orange sentinels. He handed me a chunk of pine, soft enough to forgive beginner mistakes, and showed me how to brace the knife against the seam of my thumb, how to slice away from the body in economical strokes. Within five minutes I had carved a ridge so deep the stick became topography: a canyon, a mistake, a scar. I told him I wanted to stop, that the knife felt wrong in my hand, like it had opinions. He didn’t argue, just took the half-shaped wood and turned it in his fingers until it resembled a tiny canoe. Then he set it on the railing, keel up, as if we were building miniature shipyards for ants. Years later I found that canoe in a drawer, warped by humidity, cracked along the grain, and I realized we never spoke about what the object was supposed to become; the lesson was simply to keep removing what didn’t belong.  

I want to write about night buses in foreign cities, how they turn strangers into temporary constellations. The route from Edinburgh to Glasgow at 1:13 a.m. smells of salt and fried food; a girl in the back seat silently cries into the sleeve of a university hoodie, her mascara tributaries glistening like ordinance survey lines. Two seats ahead, a man in a suit mutters spreadsheet numbers in his sleep, fingers twitching as if pressing invisible calculator keys. I am the insomniac satellite cataloging their orbits, noting how every head eventually drifts toward the window, searching for its own reflection instead of the landscape. When the lights inside the bus flare on at the final stop, we all blink like cavefish, proof that we have shared something more intimate than most lovers: collective unconsciousness powered by diesel and the willingness to let a stranger drive.  

I want to write about the smell of photocopied worksheets in elementary school, that warm toner aroma that felt like knowledge itself hot off the press. The paper always carried a faint dust that left lunar fingerprints on your palms; if you pressed your hand to your jeans fast enough, you could transfer the cosmos and walk around with a nebula on your thigh. Teachers warned us not to sniff the paper too closely—toxic chemicals, potential hallucinations—but kids courted dizziness the same way they licked nine-volt batteries for the metallic jolt. Education tasted like static; we inhaled it anyway.  

I want to write about how adulthood keeps sending meeting invites to your inner child, demanding RSVPs for performance reviews, dental cleanings, oil changes. The child never replies, just leaves crayon drawings in the margins of quarterly spreadsheets: purple dragons perched on skyscrapers, suns wearing sunglasses, stick-figure families holding hands. I keep forwarding the drawings to my future self like chain letters promising miracles if you don’t break the sequence. So far the miracles are small: a green light when I’m running late, the barista who remembers my name but not my order, so I can pretend I’m less predictable than I am.  

I want to write about the silence that grows between friends after one of you becomes a parent and the other doesn’t. It’s not resentment, exactly; more like they’ve moved to a country whose language you respect but never studied. You send care packages of jokes that reference 2003, playlists titled “Remember When,” photos of the two of you in Halloween costumes that should be embarrassing but feel archaeological. They respond with voice memos recorded in parking lots at dawn, whispering so the baby stays asleep, timestamps proof of a timezone you haven’t entered yet. You both promise to meet halfway, but halfway keeps edging farther, a horizon that obeys different physics. Still, every year on your birthday, a package arrives with tiny sneakers inside, soft-soled and alien, and you hold them to your chest like a pledge you’re afraid to interpret.  

I want to write about the dream where my departed cat returns, fur luminous, pupils dilated like blackout curtains. She speaks—not in English, but in a dialect of purrs that arrives in my bones as complete sentences: The living are only dead on a delay; fear makes the interval painful. She kneads the quilt with phantom paws, each press a small resurrection. I wake with the fabric puckered in perfect circles, impossible evidence that I decide to believe anyway, the way people believe in any rumor that promises reunion.  

I want to write about grocery store tomatoes in February, how they’re engineered for mileage instead of memory, ripened by ethylene gas instead of July. Yet sometimes, slicing one open, I find a pocket of seeds arranged like the Pleiades, and for a moment I’m barefoot in my grandmother’s garden, dirt warm against my arches, the air vibrating with cicadas. Flavor is a time machine that sometimes forgets to bring your body along; you taste the destination yet remain stranded in the produce aisle, clinging to a plastic cart that squeaks like a warning.  

I want to write about writing itself, the absurdity of arranging twenty-six shapes into a private Morse code and hoping a stranger’s pupils will dilate when they translate it. Every sentence is a dare: keep reading, I dare you to feel less alone. Sometimes I cheat by listening for the rhythm of windshield wipers, the syncopation of rain against ductwork, anything to remind me language began as percussion before it pretended to be meaning. If you tap your finger on the table while you read this, you’re playing along, keeping time for a drummer you can’t see.  

I want to write about the edge of the page rushing closer—word count ticking like a taxi meter—and how stopping feels like hanging up on someone who hasn’t finished their sentence. But maybe that’s the kindest lie we trade: that anything truly finishes. The refrigerator keeps humming, the dog starts again on its three-note aria, the screen’s black mirror shows your face lit only by the residual glow of letters that have already moved on. You close the laptop; the tide returns; the canoe waits on the railing for water it will never touch.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
